---
title: "Findings"
---

## The thing I got wrong

My first assumption was that data-dict needed the actual data to fill in
`examples` and value ranges, and that a schema on its own could never produce a
complete dictionary. I even built a version of the generator that read Parquet
files to fill those fields.

That was wrong. LinkML already has slots for all of it: `examples:` for sample
values, `minimum_value` and `maximum_value` for bounds, `permissible_values` for
enums. People just tend to leave `examples:` off because it's optional. Once the
schema actually carries those annotations, the generator produces a valid
dictionary with no data involved. Reading the data is a convenience for
auto-filling the annotations you didn't write, nothing more.

So there's no hard dependency in either direction. You can annotate the LinkML up
front and generate a complete dictionary before any data exists, or leave the
annotations thin and let a later pass over the data fill them in. Either works.

## What actually gets lost

Two things never come across, and both are on purpose.

The first is anything semantic. data-dict has no URIs, no prefixes, no ontology
mappings, so LinkML's `class_uri`, `slot_uri`, and `mappings` simply vanish.

The second is inheritance. data-dict is flat. An abstract class like
`NamedThing` produces no table of its own; its fields get copied into every
class that inherited them, and there's nothing left to say those classes were
related. The data is all still there, but the abstraction isn't.

Neither of these is a gap in LinkML. data-dict deliberately doesn't model them,
which is part of why it's a lighter format.

## The foreign key

The one spot where the two formats genuinely think differently is the foreign
key. LinkML treats a link as a reference between objects: the `territory` slot's
range is the `Territory` class. data-dict treats it as an FK column plus a
relationship with a join expression. The generator does the translation, turning
one class-valued slot into a `number(id)` column and a `relationships[]` entry.

This is also why the repo carries the data twice. `survey.data.yaml` is the
nested, object-shaped version that LinkML validates. The Parquet files are the
flat, columnar version that data-dict validates. Same rows, two shapes.

## Is data-dict a reasonable LinkML target?

I think so, with a few strings attached:

- Keep it to the relational part of the schema: flat classes, scalar and enum
  slots, FK references. A deeply nested object graph has no clean table form.
- Treat it as one-way. The semantic and inheritance losses mean you can't
  reconstruct the LinkML from the data-dict, so don't try to round-trip.
- Expect the modeler to write `examples:` and full bounds, or to accept a data
  pass later, so every column satisfies data-dict's representation rule.

Within those limits, a single LinkML schema gives you a dictionary that both
documents and validates the delivered dataset. That's a useful last-mile
companion to the code and schemas LinkML already generates.
